content:
# Leviticus 8 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Total disagreements logged:** 36 entries across all 36 verses

---

### Verse 1 — speech introduction formula and conjunction

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And the LORD spoke [vayedabber YHWH] to Moshe [el-Moshe], saying: |
| LOGOS | The LORD spoke to Moses, saying, |
| PNEUMA | The LORD spoke to Moses: [runs into v. 2 without break] |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" — LOGOS's clean formal rendering of the speech-introduction formula is adopted. The weqatal conjunction (vayedabber) is rendered narratively without "And" at the chapter opening, consistent with convention. PNEUMA's omission of "saying" (lemor) is rejected: lemor is present in the MT and represents the standard biblical Hebrew formulaic speech-introduction that should be preserved. EMET's "And" opening is appropriate for mid-narrative uses but not chapter-opening uses where English convention prefers the non-conjunctive start.

---

### Verse 2 — "Take" vs. "Bring" for qach

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Take Aharon and his sons with him..." |
| LOGOS | "Take Aaron and his sons with him..." |
| PNEUMA | "Bring Aaron and his sons with him..." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Take Aaron and his sons with him" — EMET and LOGOS agree on "Take" for qach (the standard imperative of laqach, "to take"). PNEUMA's "Bring" is a dynamic interpretation reading directional movement into a simple fetch command. While "bring" is not wrong, "take" is the more direct rendering of qach and is adopted. English names used throughout per project convention.

---

### Verse 2 — list structure (asyndeton vs. connective)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | ...the garments [ve-et-ha-begadim] and the anointing oil [ve-et shemen ha-mishchah]... [with Hebrew transliterations throughout] |
| LOGOS | ...and the garments and the anointing oil and the bull of the sin offering and the two rams and the basket of unleavened bread, |
| PNEUMA | ...along with the garments, the anointing oil, the bull for the sin offering, the two rams, and the basket of unleavened bread. |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and the garments and the anointing oil and the bull of the sin offering and the two rams and the basket of unleavened bread" — LOGOS preserves the MT's polysyndetic "and...and...and" structure (ve-et...ve-et...ve-et), which is Hebrew's standard connective list form and should be maintained in the translation. PNEUMA's comma-list with "along with" smooths this into modern English at the cost of the original's rhythmic accumulation. EMET's addition of Hebrew transliterations in brackets is noted textual commentary, not verse text — SOPHIA's verse text follows LOGOS.

---

### Verse 3 — "congregation" vs. "community"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and assemble the whole congregation [kol-ha-edah] to the entrance of the tent of meeting |
| LOGOS | and assemble all the congregation at the entrance of the tent of meeting |
| PNEUMA | Gather the whole community at the entrance of the tent of meeting |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and assemble all the congregation at the entrance of the tent of meeting" — LOGOS's rendering is adopted. "Congregation" (edah) is the established term for the cultic assembly of Israel in the priestly material — edah is a technical term for Israel assembled as a covenant community. PNEUMA's "community" is a social/sociological rendering that loses the specifically cultic-assembly sense of edah. EMET's word order places "whole congregation" after "assemble" which is slightly awkward; LOGOS's "all the congregation" is more natural. "Gather" (PNEUMA) vs. "assemble" (EMET/LOGOS): haqhel derives from qahal, "to assemble/convene a formal gathering" — "assemble" better captures the formal convening than the neutral "gather."

---

### Verse 4 — "exactly as" vs. "as"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And Moshe did as the LORD commanded him |
| LOGOS | And Moses did as the LORD commanded him |
| PNEUMA | Moses did exactly as the LORD commanded him |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And Moses did as the LORD commanded him" — EMET/LOGOS are adopted. PNEUMA adds "exactly" which is an interpretive intensifier not present in the MT's ka-asher tsivvah YHWH oto ("as the LORD commanded him"). The simple ka-asher ("as/just as") is sufficient and any intensification is an addition.

---

### Verse 5 — "This is the thing" vs. "This is what"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "This is the thing [zeh ha-davar] that the LORD has commanded to do [la'asot]." |
| LOGOS | "This is what the LORD has commanded to be done." |
| PNEUMA | "This is what the LORD has commanded to be done." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "This is what the LORD has commanded to be done." — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree, and are adopted. EMET's "This is the thing" is a maximal literalism of zeh ha-davar ("this is the word/thing") that reads awkwardly in English. The MT's davar can be rendered "thing" or "word" or summarized as "what" in English. "This is what" is both accurate and natural.

---

### Verse 6 — "brought near" vs. "brought forward"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And Moshe brought near [vayaqrev] Aharon and his sons |
| LOGOS | Then Moses brought Aaron and his sons forward |
| PNEUMA | Moses brought Aaron and his sons forward |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Then Moses brought Aaron and his sons forward" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "brought forward" which is more natural English for the presentational gesture of hiqriv. EMET's "brought near" is a literal rendering of the verbal root qrv ("to draw near / approach") but reads awkwardly as an English idiom for presenting someone to a ceremony. "Forward" captures the directional presentation sense more naturally.

---

### Verse 7 — decorated band description

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and girded him with the decorated band of the ephod [be-cheshev ha-ephod] and bound it to him with it |
| LOGOS | and tied the skillfully woven band of the ephod around him, binding it to him |
| PNEUMA | He placed the ephod on him and fastened it with the decorated band, tying the ephod close to his body. |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and tied the decorated band of the ephod around him, binding it to him" — EMET and PNEUMA agree on "decorated band" for cheshev ha-ephod. LOGOS's "skillfully woven" is an expansion drawn from the fuller description in Exodus 28:8 (cheshev ... ma'aseh oregh, "the band ... work of a weaver"), not from the MT text of Lev 8:7 which simply reads "the band of the ephod." "Decorated" is retained as a minimal descriptive that accurately reflects the object without importing the Exodus detail. PNEUMA's "tying the ephod close to his body" is an explanatory expansion (not in MT) and is rejected. EMET's "bound it to him with it" (ve-ye'ephod lo bo) is a correct literal rendering of the reflexive fastening idiom but is awkward; SOPHIA adopts "binding it to him" (LOGOS) as cleaner.

---

### Verse 8 — Urim and Thummim spelling

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | the Urim and the Tummim [et-ha-urim ve-et-ha-tummim] |
| LOGOS | the Urim and Thummim |
| PNEUMA | the Urim and the Thummim |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "the Urim and the Thummim" — PNEUMA's rendering, with the definite article before both terms (reflecting the MT's ha-urim ve-ha-tummim) and the conventional double-H spelling (Thummim). EMET's single-H "Tummim" is a more phonetically precise transliteration of the Hebrew Tummim but departs from the well-established English convention. LOGOS omits the article before "Thummim" inconsistently. SOPHIA preserves the article before both terms and adopts the standard English spelling.

---

### Verse 9 — nezer ha-qodesh rendering

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | the golden plate [tsits ha-zahav], the holy diadem [nezer ha-qodesh] |
| LOGOS | the golden plate, the holy crown |
| PNEUMA | the golden ornament — the sacred diadem |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "the golden plate, the holy crown" — LOGOS is adopted. EMET's "holy diadem" is accurate but less widely understood. PNEUMA's "golden ornament" significantly underdescribes the tsits (which is a plate/rosette of beaten gold, inscribed "Holy to the LORD," not a generic "ornament") and "sacred diadem" duplicates the sense without adding precision. "The holy crown" follows the majority of English translations (ESV, KJV) and correctly captures nezer's dual sense of royal diadem and consecration-emblem. The em-dash in PNEUMA is also a structural divergence from the comma apposition used by EMET and LOGOS; comma is adopted.

---

### Verse 10 — "Then Moses" vs. "And Moses"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And Moshe took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and all that was in it and consecrated them. |
| LOGOS | Moses then took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and all that was in it, and consecrated them. |
| PNEUMA | Moses then took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and everything in it, consecrating them. |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Moses then took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and all that was in it, and consecrated them." — LOGOS is adopted. The use of "then" as a narrative sequencer (vs. EMET's "And") appropriately marks the new section at v. 10. PNEUMA's "everything in it" is slightly less precise than "all that was in it" (kol-asher-bo) but substantively equivalent; SOPHIA follows LOGOS for consistency. PNEUMA's participial "consecrating them" converts the MT's coordinate main clause (vayiqaddesh otam = "and he consecrated them") into a subordinate participle, changing the syntactic weight of the consecration act — rejected.

---

### Verse 11 — "utensils" vs. "vessels"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and anointed the altar and all its vessels [ve-et-kol-kelav] |
| LOGOS | and anointed the altar and all its utensils |
| PNEUMA | anointing the altar and all its utensils |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and anointed the altar and all its utensils" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree. "Utensils" is adopted over EMET's "vessels" — keli can mean vessel or utensil broadly, but in the context of altar equipment (fire pans, shovels, basins, forks, etc.) "utensils" is the more fitting English rendering. "Vessels" more typically implies containers; the altar's keilim are primarily tools and implements.

---

### Verse 12 — "setting him apart as holy" vs. "to consecrate him"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and anointed him to consecrate him [le-qaddesh oto] |
| LOGOS | and anointed him, to consecrate him |
| PNEUMA | and anointed him, setting him apart as holy |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and anointed him, to consecrate him" — EMET and LOGOS agree, and are adopted. PNEUMA's "setting him apart as holy" is an expansion that paraphrases qaddesh ("to consecrate") with two words where one is sufficient. The project uses "consecrate" as the standard rendering for the piel of qadash throughout.

---

### Verse 13 — migba'ot: "turbans" vs. "caps" vs. "headbands"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and bound turbans on them [vayachbosh lahem migba'ot] |
| LOGOS | and bound caps on them |
| PNEUMA | and bound headbands on them |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and bound turbans on them" — EMET is adopted. The migba'ot (plural of migba'ah) are the headcoverings of the ordinary priests, distinct from the high priest's mitsnefet but analogous in form — wound cloth headcoverings. "Turbans" (EMET, ESV, NASB) is the standard English rendering. "Caps" (LOGOS, NIV tradition) and "headbands" (PNEUMA) both underrepresent the form of these wound garments. The verb chabhash ("to bind/wind") used for the migba'ot also supports a wound-cloth interpretation over a simple cap or headband.

---

### Verse 14 — "brought" vs. "presented"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And he brought near the bull of the sin offering |
| LOGOS | Then he brought the bull of the sin offering |
| PNEUMA | He brought the bull for the sin offering |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Then he brought the bull of the sin offering" — LOGOS is adopted. EMET's "brought near" again renders hiqriv maximally literally; "brought" is natural English. PNEUMA's "for the sin offering" (vs. "of the sin offering") introduces a purpose reading of par ha-chattat where the MT's construct chain ("bull of the sin offering") is a genitive of classification, not purpose. "Of the sin offering" is the more precise rendering.

---

### Verse 15 — "all around" vs. "around about" + blood procedure

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and put on the horns of the altar around about [saviv] with his finger and purified the altar and the blood he poured at the base of the altar and consecrated it to make atonement over it |
| LOGOS | and with his finger put it on the horns of the altar around about, and purified the altar, and poured out the blood at the base of the altar and consecrated it to make atonement for it |
| PNEUMA | With his finger he put it on all four horns of the altar, purifying the altar, then poured out the remaining blood at the base of the altar. So he consecrated it and made atonement for it. |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and with his finger put it on the horns of the altar all around, and purified the altar, and poured out the blood at the base of the altar and consecrated it, to make atonement for it" — LOGOS's structure is closely followed. Three items rejected from PNEUMA: (1) "all four horns" — the MT reads simply "the horns of the altar around about" (qarnot ha-mizbeach saviv); "four" is not specified in this verse and is an interpretation imported from Ex 27:2 / Lev 4:7; (2) "remaining blood" — "the blood" (et-ha-dam) with no qualifier; (3) "So he consecrated it and made atonement for it" — the MT's le-khapper alav is an infinitive of purpose ("to make atonement for/over it"), not a coordinate act; SOPHIA renders the purpose clause with "to make atonement for it." "Over it" (EMET, le-khapper alav) vs. "for it" (LOGOS): le-khapper alav uses the preposition al which can mean "over" or "for/on behalf of"; "for it" (referring to the altar) is cleaner English.

---

### Verse 16 — "entrails" vs. "inner organs"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | all the fat that was on the entrails [asher al-ha-qerev] |
| LOGOS | all the fat that was on the entrails |
| PNEUMA | all the fat that was on the inner organs |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "all the fat that was on the entrails" — EMET and LOGOS agree. "Entrails" is the established rendering for qerev ("inward parts, innards") throughout the Levitical series. PNEUMA's "inner organs" is not wrong but introduces a different register; consistency with the established rendering is maintained.

---

### Verse 17 — "hide" vs. "skin"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | But the bull and its skin [ve-et-oro] and its flesh and its dung he burned with fire outside the camp |
| LOGOS | But the bull and its hide and its flesh and its dung he burned up with fire outside the camp |
| PNEUMA | But the bull itself — its hide, its flesh, and its dung — he burned in the fire outside the camp |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "But the bull and its hide and its flesh and its dung he burned with fire outside the camp" — LOGOS is adopted for "hide" over EMET's "skin." The MT's or is the animal's outer covering; "hide" is more precise for a large bovine. PNEUMA's "the bull itself" (the MT has simply ve-et ha-par, "and the bull") and the em-dash structure introduce emphasis and punctuation not present in the MT. PNEUMA's "burned in the fire" vs. "burned with fire" (EMET/LOGOS): the MT's saraf ba-esh is "he burned with fire" — the instrument ba-esh should be rendered "with fire," not reduced to "in the fire." LOGOS's "burned up" adds a completeness nuance (saraf alone means to burn; "burned up" implies total incineration), which is a valid implication but slightly beyond the MT.

---

### Verse 18 — "presented" vs. "brought near" / "he then" sentence structure

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And he brought near [vayaqrev] the ram of the burnt offering |
| LOGOS | Then he presented the ram of the burnt offering |
| PNEUMA | He then presented the ram for the burnt offering |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Then he presented the ram of the burnt offering" — LOGOS is adopted. As at v. 14: "presented" is more natural than "brought near" for hiqriv in a sacrificial presentation context. PNEUMA's "for the burnt offering" again introduces purpose where the MT's construct genitive "of the burnt offering" (eil ha-olah) is classificatory.

---

### Verse 19 — "threw" vs. "dashed" for zraq

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and Moshe dashed [vayizroq Moshe] the blood against the altar on all sides [saviv] |
| LOGOS | and Moses threw the blood against the altar on all sides |
| PNEUMA | Moses slaughtered it and threw the blood against all sides of the altar |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and Moses threw the blood against the altar on all sides" — LOGOS is adopted. EMET's "dashed" is a legitimate rendering of zraq ("to toss/throw/dash with force") that is used by some English Bibles (NASB) and has the advantage of capturing the forceful, projecting nature of the gesture as distinct from the finger-application of v. 15. However, "threw" is more natural as modern English and has been established in this project's prior chapters. PNEUMA's phrase order ("against all sides of the altar" vs. "against the altar on all sides") is acceptable but less close to the MT's structure (al-ha-mizbeach saviv — "against the altar, around").

---

### Verse 20 — subject of cutting

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And the ram he cut [nitteach] into its pieces and Moshe burned the head and the pieces and the fat |
| LOGOS | He cut the ram into pieces, and Moses burned the head and the pieces and the fat |
| PNEUMA | He cut the ram into pieces and burned the head, the pieces, and the fat |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "He cut the ram into pieces, and Moses burned the head and the pieces and the fat" — LOGOS is adopted. EMET's fronted object ("the ram he cut") is a Hebrew topicalization that is unnatural in English; LOGOS's word order is preferred. PNEUMA merges the subject between cutting and burning without naming Moses — the MT switches explicitly from "he" (Moses) to "vayaqter Moshe" (Moses burned), so naming Moses at the second verb is correct. PNEUMA's comma-list format also differs from the MT's simple "and...and...and" structure.

---

### Verse 21 — "for a pleasing aroma" vs. "with a pleasing aroma"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | it is a burnt offering [olah hu] for a pleasing aroma [le-re'ach nichoach], a fire offering [isheh] to the LORD |
| LOGOS | It was a burnt offering with a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD |
| PNEUMA | It was a burnt offering, a fire offering, a pleasing aroma — an offering made by fire to the LORD |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "It was a burnt offering for a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD" — EMET's preposition "for" (le-re'ach nichoach = "for/as a pleasing aroma") is preferred over LOGOS's "with." The le- preposition indicates the character or purpose of the offering's aroma ("as/for a pleasing aroma"), not a secondary accompanying characteristic ("with"). PNEUMA's reordering (burnt offering, fire offering, pleasing aroma) inverts the MT sequence (olah, re'ach nichoach, isheh) and is rejected. PNEUMA also adds "made by fire" for isheh — unnecessary expansion; "fire offering" is sufficient. EMET's present-tense "it is" (reflecting the Hebrew olah hu with gapping) vs. LOGOS's past-tense "it was": since the narrative is past, "it was" is preferred.

---

### Verse 22 — "second ram" vs. "other ram"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And he brought near the second ram [ha-ayil ha-sheni], the ram of ordination [eil ha-millu'im] |
| LOGOS | Then he presented the other ram, the ram of ordination |
| PNEUMA | He then presented the second ram — the ram of ordination |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Then he presented the second ram, the ram of ordination" — PNEUMA is adopted for "second" over LOGOS's "other." The MT has ha-ayil ha-sheni, "the second ram" — a specific ordinal reference back to the two rams listed in v. 2. "The other" (LOGOS) is a looser rendering that loses the ordinal precision. PNEUMA's em-dash is replaced with a comma for consistency with SOPHIA's verse style.

---

### Verse 23 — tenukh ozen: "lobe" anatomical precision

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | the lobe of the right ear of Aharon [al-tenukh ozen Aharon ha-yemanit] |
| LOGOS | the lobe of Aaron's right ear |
| PNEUMA | the lobe of Aaron's right ear |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "the lobe of Aaron's right ear" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree and are adopted. EMET's word order ("the right ear of Aaron") is more literal but less natural. "Lobe" is correct for tenukh (the cartilage tip/lobe) — all three agents agree on this precise anatomical rendering, correctly rejecting the generic "ear" of some translations.

---

### Verse 23 — sentence structure: blood application sequence

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and put it on the lobe of the right ear of Aharon and upon the thumb of his right hand and upon the big toe of his right foot |
| LOGOS | and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot |
| PNEUMA | and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot" — LOGOS is adopted. The MT's polysyndetic structure (ve-al...ve-al...ve-al, "and on...and on...and on") is preserved with repeated "and on" rather than PNEUMA's comma-list. EMET's "upon" is a slightly formal variant of "on" that is acceptable but less consistent with the project's conventions.

---

### Verse 24 — collective singular vs. plural for "ear/ears"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | upon the lobe of their right ear [al-tenukh oznam ha-yemanit — collective singular] |
| LOGOS | on the lobes of their right ears |
| PNEUMA | on the lobe of each one's right ear |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "on the lobe of their right ear" — EMET is adopted. The MT uses a collective singular (oznam, "their ear" not "their ears"), consistent with Hebrew's use of a singular collective for body parts shared by a group. LOGOS's naturalized English plural ("ears") smooths the Hebrew idiom but loses the collective form. PNEUMA's "each one's right ear" is interpretively accurate but adds words not present in the MT.

---

### Verse 24 — "rest of the blood" vs. "the blood"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and Moshe dashed the blood against the altar on all sides |
| LOGOS | and Moses threw the rest of the blood against the altar on all sides |
| PNEUMA | Moses then threw the remaining blood against all sides of the altar |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and Moses threw the blood against the altar on all sides" — EMET is adopted. The MT reads ve-et-ha-dam yizroq Moshe al-ha-mizbeach saviv — simply "and the blood Moses threw against the altar around." LOGOS and PNEUMA both add "rest of" / "remaining" — a reasonable logical inference (since some blood has been used for the ear-thumb-toe application) but not present in the MT text and therefore not adopted in the translation.

---

### Verse 25 — fat tail (alyah) description

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | the fat [et-ha-chelev] and the fat tail [ve-et-ha-alyah] and all the fat that was on the entrails |
| LOGOS | the fat and the fat tail and all the fat that was on the entrails |
| PNEUMA | the fat — the fat tail, all the fat around the inner organs |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "the fat and the fat tail and all the fat that was on the entrails" — EMET and LOGOS agree and are adopted. PNEUMA's dash structure and "fat around the inner organs" (vs. "fat that was on the entrails") are both departures: the MT's asher al-ha-qerev is "that was on the entrails" (surface fat on the gut cavity), not "around the inner organs." "Entrails" is maintained over PNEUMA's "inner organs" for consistency (see v. 16 note).

---

### Verse 26 — bread order

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | one unleavened loaf [challat matsah achat] and one loaf of bread with oil [ve-challat lechem shemen achat] and one wafer [ve-rekik echad] |
| LOGOS | one unleavened loaf and one loaf of bread with oil and one wafer |
| PNEUMA | one unleavened loaf, one loaf of bread made with oil, and one wafer |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "one unleavened loaf and one loaf of bread with oil and one wafer" — EMET and LOGOS agree and are adopted. PNEUMA's "made with oil" expands shemen ("of oil" / "with oil") slightly; the MT has challat lechem shemen ("a loaf, bread of oil"), where shemen is a construct-genitive indicating the oil content. "With oil" (EMET/LOGOS) is the more compact and equally accurate rendering.

---

### Verse 27 — "palms" vs. "hands"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And he placed all of it on the palms of Aharon [al-kapey Aharon] and on the palms of his sons [ve-al kapey vanav] |
| LOGOS | And he put all of it in the hands of Aaron and in the hands of his sons |
| PNEUMA | He put it all in Aaron's hands and in the hands of his sons |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And he put all of it on the palms of Aaron and on the palms of his sons" — EMET's "palms" is adopted over LOGOS's "hands." The MT's kappayim (dual of kaph, "palm, hollow of the hand") is more specific than the general "hands" (yadayim). The image of the ordination portions being placed on the cupped palms — a gesture of presentation and receiving — is theologically significant and should be preserved by the more specific "palms." PNEUMA's compression of the phrase is also adopted partially: "he put" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over EMET's "he placed" — both are acceptable but SOPHIA maintains "put" for consistency.

---

### Verse 28 — "ordination offering" formula

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | they are an ordination offering [millu'im hem] for a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD |
| LOGOS | They were an ordination offering with a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD |
| PNEUMA | This was the ordination offering — a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "They were an ordination offering for a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD" — EMET's "for" (le-re'ach nichoach, as at v. 21) and LOGOS's plural/past "They were" are combined. PNEUMA's inverted word order (fire offering before pleasing aroma) departs from the MT sequence (millu'im...re'ach nichoach...isheh) and is rejected. PNEUMA's em-dash is also rejected for consistency.

---

### Verse 29 — "Moses's portion" word order and possessive

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | from the ram of ordination it was Moshe's portion [hayah le-Moshe le-manah] |
| LOGOS | It was Moses' portion of the ram of ordination |
| PNEUMA | It was Moses's own portion from the ram of ordination |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "it was Moses's portion from the ram of ordination" — PNEUMA's word order is slightly preferred but "own" (not in MT) is dropped. The MT has me-eil ha-millu'im ("from the ram of ordination"), so "from" is more accurate than LOGOS's "of." LOGOS's "Moses'" (single apostrophe) vs. PNEUMA's "Moses's" (apostrophe-s): both are grammatically acceptable; "Moses's" is adopted as per the convention followed in the earlier chapter files. EMET's fronted "from the ram of ordination" is the MT's actual phrase order (the whole verse ends with me-eil ha-millu'im) but reads awkwardly in English.

---

### Verse 30 — pronoun clarity: "it" vs. "them" for mixed substance

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | and sprinkled on Aharon and on his garments and on his sons and on the garments of his sons with him and he consecrated Aharon and his garments and his sons and the garments of his sons with him |
| LOGOS | and sprinkled it on Aaron and his garments, and also on his sons and his sons' garments. So he consecrated Aaron and his garments, and his sons and his sons' garments with him. |
| PNEUMA | and sprinkled it on Aaron and on his garments, and on his sons and on their garments. In this way he consecrated Aaron and his garments, and his sons and their garments with him. |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and sprinkled it on Aaron and his garments, and on his sons and his sons' garments. So he consecrated Aaron and his garments, and his sons and his sons' garments with him." — LOGOS is adopted with minor adjustments. EMET's run-on structure (all one sentence) is less readable. PNEUMA's "In this way" is an interpretive connector ("In this way" implies the sprinkling was the mechanism of consecration); SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "So" which is a natural narrative connector without over-specifying the logical relationship. PNEUMA uses "their garments" in the first clause but "their garments" in the second — LOGOS consistently uses "his sons' garments" which matches the MT (bigdey vanav ito, "garments of his sons with him"); "his sons' garments" is adopted throughout.

---

### Verse 31 — "Boil" vs. "Cook" + basket description

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Boil the flesh at the entrance of the tent of meeting and there eat it and the bread that is in the basket of ordination [asher be-sal ha-millu'im] |
| LOGOS | "Boil the flesh at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and eat it there with the bread that is in the basket of ordination offerings |
| PNEUMA | "Boil the meat at the entrance of the tent of meeting and eat it there, along with the bread in the basket of ordination offerings |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Boil the flesh at the entrance of the tent of meeting and eat it there with the bread that is in the basket of ordination offerings" — LOGOS is adopted. (1) "Flesh" vs. "meat": the MT has basar, which means flesh/meat; "flesh" is slightly more literal and maintains consistency with the ritual vocabulary (flesh = basar is an important cultic term). PNEUMA's "meat" is a natural English word but loses the cultic register. (2) "Basket of ordination offerings" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "basket of ordination" (EMET): the MT's sal ha-millu'im is "basket of the ordination(s)"; "ordination offerings" specifies what is in the basket (the bread offerings of the ordination ceremony) and is a clarifying but acceptable rendering. SOPHIA follows LOGOS. (3) PNEUMA's "along with" introduces a slightly different connective structure than the MT's simple ve- ("and").

---

### Verse 32 — "burn up" vs. "burn"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And what remains of the flesh and of the bread you shall burn with fire [ba-esh tisrofu] |
| LOGOS | And what remains of the flesh and the bread you shall burn up with fire |
| PNEUMA | Whatever remains of the meat and the bread, burn it in the fire |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And what remains of the flesh and of the bread you shall burn with fire" — EMET is adopted. LOGOS's "burn up" adds a completeness implication not strictly in the MT's saraf ba-esh ("burn with fire"). PNEUMA's "in the fire" (vs. "with fire") changes the instrument preposition; "with fire" (ba-esh) is more accurate. PNEUMA's "burn it" adds a pronoun not in the MT's direct address form. PNEUMA's "Whatever" is a natural English rendering of ve-ha-notar ("and the remainder") but the "and" coordinator is part of the narrative flow and should be preserved.

---

### Verse 33 — "ordain you" vs. "fill your hands" idiom

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | until the day of the completion of the days of your ordination — for seven days he shall ordain you [yemalle et-yedekhem — lit. "he shall fill your hands"] |
| LOGOS | until the days of your ordination are completed, for it will take seven days to ordain you |
| PNEUMA | not until the days of your ordination are complete. For your ordination will take seven days |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "until the days of your ordination are completed — for seven days he shall ordain you" — EMET's "he shall ordain you" is adopted over LOGOS's impersonal "it will take seven days to ordain you" and PNEUMA's nominalized "your ordination will take seven days." The MT's yemalle et-yedekhem is a third-person verbal form ("he shall fill your hands") that preserves the agency of the ordaining one; making it impersonal or nominal suppresses this. "He shall ordain you" captures the idiom's function while the literal "fill your hands" is preserved in the Council Notes.

---

### Verse 34 — "As has been done today" vs. "As has been done on this day"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | As has been done on this day [ba-yom ha-zeh], the LORD commanded to do to make atonement for you |
| LOGOS | As has been done today, the LORD has commanded to be done to make atonement for you |
| PNEUMA | What has been done today, the LORD commanded to be done in order to make atonement for you |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "As has been done today, the LORD commanded to be done, to make atonement for you" — LOGOS is closely followed. EMET's "on this day" is more literal (ba-yom ha-zeh = "on this/the day") but "today" is the natural idiomatic English equivalent and is not a loss of meaning. PNEUMA's "What has been done" shifts from the comparative "as/just as" (ka-asher) to a nominal clause; the comparative structure should be preserved. LOGOS's "has commanded" (perfect) vs. EMET's "commanded" (simple past): the context is Moses narrating the divine instruction as past fact — "commanded" (simple past) is preferred, following EMET. The infinitive "to make atonement for you" (le-khapper aleykhem) is a purpose clause and is rendered accordingly.

---

### Verse 35 — "remain" vs. "stay" + death warning

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And at the entrance of the tent of meeting you shall remain [teshvu] day and night for seven days and you shall keep the charge of the LORD that you may not die — for so I was commanded |
| LOGOS | At the entrance of the tent of meeting you shall remain day and night for seven days, keeping the LORD's charge, so that you do not die, for so I have been commanded |
| PNEUMA | You must remain at the entrance of the tent of meeting day and night for seven days and keep the LORD's charge, so that you do not die. For that is what I have been commanded. |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "At the entrance of the tent of meeting you shall remain day and night for seven days, keeping the LORD's charge, so that you do not die — for so I have been commanded." — LOGOS is closely adopted. (1) PNEUMA's "You must remain" introduces a modal obligation stronger than the MT's second-person imperfect teshvu ("you shall dwell/remain"). "You shall remain" is preferred. (2) LOGOS's participial "keeping the LORD's charge" vs. EMET's coordinate "and you shall keep the charge": the MT has u-shemartem ("and you shall keep"), a coordinate verb, not a participial — EMET is technically more accurate but LOGOS's participial reads more naturally. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's rendering. (3) "so that you do not die" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "that you may not die" (EMET): "so that you do not die" is preferred as a purpose-clause rendering consistent with the LXX's hina mē apothanēte. (4) PNEUMA's "For that is what I have been commanded" is a rephrasing of ki-khen tsuvveiti ("for so I was/have been commanded") — LOGOS's "for so I have been commanded" preserves the Hebrew structure more exactly.

---

### Verse 36 — compliance formula: "all the things" vs. "everything" + mediatorial preposition

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | And Aharon and his sons did all the things that the LORD commanded through the hand of Moshe |
| LOGOS | And Aaron and his sons did all the things that the LORD commanded by Moses |
| PNEUMA | Aaron and his sons did everything the LORD had commanded through Moses |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And Aaron and his sons did all the things that the LORD commanded through Moses." — A synthesis is adopted: EMET/LOGOS's "all the things" (kol-ha-devarim) over PNEUMA's compressed "everything," and EMET/PNEUMA's "through Moses" over LOGOS's "by Moses." "Through Moses" better captures the be-yad Moshe ("by/through the hand of Moses") mediation-of-agency idiom — the LORD's command is transmitted through Moses as agent and instrument. LOGOS's "by" is grammatically correct but "through" more precisely renders the spatial/instrumental force of be-yad ("by the hand of"). PNEUMA's "had commanded" (past perfect) vs. EMET/LOGOS's "commanded" (simple past): the MT uses a simple past (tsivvah), so "commanded" is retained.

---

*SOPHIA — disagreement log for Leviticus 8, synthesized from EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), and PNEUMA (dynamic)*
